history.text: As a response to onslaughts by Avars, the local Slavic tribes rebelled and established Samo's Empire (623–658), the first known Slavic political entity.

history.label: 9th century
history.text: In the 9th century, the castles at Bratislava (Brezalauspurk) and Devín (Dowina) were important centres of the Slavic states: the Principality of Nitra and Great Moravia.

history.label: 10th century
history.text: In the 10th century, the territory of Pressburg (what would later become Pozsony county) became part of Hungary (called the "Kingdom of Hungary" from 1000).
The medieval settlement Brezalauspurc (literally 'Braslav's castle') was first mentioned in 907, and is attributed to Bratislava, but the actual location of Brezalauspurc is under scholarly debate.
Owing to Ottoman advances into Hungarian territory, the city was designated the new capital of Hungary in 1536, after becoming part of the Habsburg monarchy and marking the beginning of a new era.
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart gave a concert in 1762 in the Pálffy Palace.
The first horse-drawn railway in the Kingdom of Hungary, from Pressburg to Szentgyörgy (Svätý Jur), was built in 1840.
After World War I, during the dissolution of Austria-Hungary, between 1918 and 1919, the name Wilsonov or Wilsonstadt was proposed by Slovak Americans.
In 1993, Bratislava once again became the capital of the newly formed independent Slovak Republic, following the Velvet Divorce.
The first known permanent settlement of the area began with the Linear Pottery Culture, around 5000 B.C. in the Neolithic era.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: About 200 B.C., the Celtic Boii tribe founded the first significant settlement, a fortified town known as the Bratislava oppidum.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Scholars have debated the identification as fortresses of the two castles built in Great Moravia, based on linguistic arguments and because of the absence of convincing archaeological evidence.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Pressburg flourished during the 18th-century reign of Queen Maria Theresa, becoming the largest and most important town in the Kingdom of Hungary.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: After World War II, Slovak Republic lost its so-called independence and was reunified again with the Czech Republic as Czechoslovak Republic.
